turns.content:
Hi! I'm here to help you get your project off to a strong start. I'll ask you a few questions to understand what you're building, and then I'll generate some foundational project documents for you.

To kick things off — **what's the project about?** Give me the big picture: what does it do, and why are you building it?

Bop is a multi-persona code review agent written in go.  It is deeply integrated with github, and can perform code reviews on commits to a PR in github actions, and post review finding comments to the PR exactly as a senior code reviewer on the development team would.

That's a compelling concept — bringing structured, persona-driven code review directly into the GitHub Actions workflow. I love the "senior code reviewer on the team" framing.

When you say **multi-persona**, what do you mean exactly? Are these different reviewer archetypes (e.g., a security expert, a performance engineer, a style enforcer), or something else — like different configurations per team or project?

there are is a default persona that ships with bop that will do a decent job.  Personas are configurable.  You can configure the prompt, the model, the weight of the reviewer's findings, and whether it is one of the default reviewers in a review panel.  A project can include a bop.yaml config file to specify their reviewer panel configuration.  For example, this project has multiple non-default reviewer personas configured - including security, architecture, and performance reviewers.

That's a really nice design — a configurable review panel with weighted findings, shipped with sensible defaults but fully customizable per project via `bop.yaml`.

A few things click into place from that. Let me dig into the review output: when Bop posts findings to a GitHub PR, does it post **as a single unified review** (synthesizing across all personas), or does **each persona post separately** — like distinct review comments attributed to each reviewer?

Indeed it does synthesize the findings from all reviewers, and goes further with multi-layer deduplication, including sematic deduplication and confidence scoring of all findings.  Subsequent reviews include previous round findings when instructing reviewers not to repeat themselves, also.  Once the final synthesized review for a given round is established, those findings get posted to the PR.

That's a sophisticated pipeline — the semantic deduplication and confidence scoring especially add real value over a naive "just concatenate all reviews" approach. And threading previous round context into subsequent reviews is a nice touch for iterative PRs.

That leads me to ask about the **LLM backend** — since personas are configurable with a model setting, is Bop designed to work with multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.), or is there a primary provider it targets, with others as stretch goals?
It works with Anthropic, Google, OpenAI, and Ollama.  The default reviewer uses claude sonnet.

Great — solid provider coverage, including local models via Ollama which is a nice touch for teams with data privacy concerns.

Now, thinking about the GitHub Actions integration — how does Bop actually run? Is it a **single binary** that gets invoked as a step in a workflow, a Docker container action, or something else? And does it need any persistent storage, or is each review run essentially stateless?

it is a statically compile go binary that gets invoked during a step in the github action.  It leverages comment metadata to persist bop-specific info across review rounds, as well as uses the previously posted comments.  Otherwise, it is stateless and acts on the diff.

Clever use of GitHub's own comment metadata as a persistence layer — no external database needed, keeps the architecture clean and the binary truly stateless between runs.

On the GitHub integration side, what does Bop need in terms of permissions and authentication? I'm assuming it needs a GitHub token to post comments — is it designed to work with the standard `GITHUB_TOKEN` that Actions provides automatically, or does it require a dedicated GitHub App or PAT with broader permissions?

It is designed to use the GITHUB_TOKEN in the github actions context.  It does need a couple of additional permissions in the worflow that I don't recall off the top of my head.  There is an example github actions workflow in the repo though, which contains that info.  I forgot to mention that bop can be run on your local machine to review either a local checkout OR a remote PR.  There is also an MCP serverso all bop's capabilities can be driven from any MCP compliant coding assistant.  When bop is run locally (or via MCP) it will require a GITHUB_TOKEN to be set in the environment.  Also bop requires LLM API keys for each provider/model being used by bop.

That's a rich set of deployment modes — GitHub Actions, local CLI, and MCP server all from the same binary. Very versatile.

Let me ask about the **MCP server** specifically — is it a separate binary/command, or is it a subcommand of the same `bop` binary (e.g., `bop mcp serve`)? And roughly what capabilities does it expose — just triggering reviews, or also things like listing personas, inspecting findings, etc.?

it is a separate binary (bop-mcp).  It possesses all the core capabilities of bop cli (they are at parity).  It can retrieve findings, post responses, dismiss reviews (or rerequest a review).  It can perform code reviews with the bop agent.  Everything the cli can do.

Clean separation with two binaries sharing a core library — that's a solid Go monorepo pattern.

Who is the **target user** for Bop? Is this primarily aimed at open source projects, engineering teams at companies, individual developers, or some mix? And related to that — is this going to be open source itself, and who owns it?

It is aimed (generically) at anyone who wants a senior code reviewer looking at their code, that can keep up with the pace an AI coding assistant is capable of generating code.  Initially, I imagined solo devs would be drawn to it.  As time has passed, I have come to believe it's more likely small teams focused on shipping.  But large teams/enterprise team would also find it useful.  It is an open source project.  It lives in the delightfulhammers org space in Gitub.

"Keeping up with the pace an AI coding assistant generates code" — that's a sharp positioning statement, very timely.

We're getting close to having everything I need. One last area: what does **"done" look like for the initial version**? You may already have a working product — if so, what's the current state, and is there a v1.0 milestone or set of capabilities you're targeting that isn't complete yet?
it's currently v0.12.3, which is very feature complete, and in front of beta testers.  I don't have a v1.0 milestone defined at this time.

That's helpful context — so this is a mature, feature-complete project heading toward a stable v1.0 release rather than a greenfield build. The documents will reflect that.

One final question: are there things that are **explicitly out of scope** for Bop — either features you've consciously decided not to build, or platforms/integrations you're not targeting? For example, GitLab or Bitbucket support, auto-fixing code, CI systems other than GitHub Actions, etc.?
I have tracking issues for most of those example features you mention, but they are not planned out yet.  Auto-fixing code was not in scope.  Your other examples were.

That's clear — auto-fixing code is the firm out-of-scope item, and things like GitLab/Bitbucket support and other CI systems are on the radar but unplanned. Good to know the distinction.

I have everything I need to generate solid project documents. Let me put those together for you now.

```json
{"interviewComplete": true}
```
